In [1]:
!jupyter nbconvert --to html /content/EE243project.ipynb

[NbConvertApp] WARNING | pattern '/content/EE243project.ipynb' matched no files
This application is used to convert notebook files (*.ipynb)
        to various other formats.

        WARNING: THE COMMANDLINE INTERFACE MAY CHANGE IN FUTURE RELEASES.

Options
=======
The options below are convenience aliases to configurable class-options,
as listed in the "Equivalent to" description-line of the aliases.
To see all configurable class-options for some <cmd>, use:
    <cmd> --help-all

--debug
    set log level to logging.DEBUG (maximize logging output)
    Equivalent to: [--Application.log_level=10]
--show-config
    Show the application's configuration (human-readable format)
    Equivalent to: [--Application.show_config=True]
--show-config-json
    Show the application's configuration (json format)
    Equivalent to: [--Application.show_config_json=True]
--generate-config
    generate default config file
    Equivalent to: [--JupyterApp.generate_config=True]
-y
    Answer yes to any questions instead of prompting.
    Equivalent to: [--JupyterApp.answer_yes=True]
--execute
    Execute the notebook prior to export.
    Equivalent to: [--ExecutePreprocessor.enabled=True]
--allow-errors
    Continue notebook execution even if one of the cells throws an error and include the error message in the cell output (the default behaviour is to abort conversion). This flag is only relevant if '--execute' was specified, too.
    Equivalent to: [--ExecutePreprocessor.allow_errors=True]
--stdin
    read a single notebook file from stdin. Write the resulting notebook with default basename 'notebook.*'
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.from_stdin=True]
--stdout
    Write notebook output to stdout instead of files.
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.writer_class=StdoutWriter]
--inplace
    Run nbconvert in place, overwriting the existing notebook (only
            relevant when converting to notebook format)
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.use_output_suffix=False --NbConvertApp.export_format=notebook --FilesWriter.build_directory=]
--clear-output
    Clear output of current file and save in place,
            overwriting the existing notebook.
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.use_output_suffix=False --NbConvertApp.export_format=notebook --FilesWriter.build_directory= --ClearOutputPreprocessor.enabled=True]
--coalesce-streams
    Coalesce consecutive stdout and stderr outputs into one stream (within each cell).
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.use_output_suffix=False --NbConvertApp.export_format=notebook --FilesWriter.build_directory= --CoalesceStreamsPreprocessor.enabled=True]
--no-prompt
    Exclude input and output prompts from converted document.
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.exclude_input_prompt=True --TemplateExporter.exclude_output_prompt=True]
--no-input
    Exclude input cells and output prompts from converted document.
            This mode is ideal for generating code-free reports.
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.exclude_output_prompt=True --TemplateExporter.exclude_input=True --TemplateExporter.exclude_input_prompt=True]
--allow-chromium-download
    Whether to allow downloading chromium if no suitable version is found on the system.
    Equivalent to: [--WebPDFExporter.allow_chromium_download=True]
--disable-chromium-sandbox
    Disable chromium security sandbox when converting to PDF..
    Equivalent to: [--WebPDFExporter.disable_sandbox=True]
--show-input
    Shows code input. This flag is only useful for dejavu users.
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.exclude_input=False]
--embed-images
    Embed the images as base64 dataurls in the output. This flag is only useful for the HTML/WebPDF/Slides exports.
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.embed_images=True]
--sanitize-html
    Whether the HTML in Markdown cells and cell outputs should be sanitized..
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.sanitize_html=True]
--log-level=<Enum>
    Set the log level by value or name.
    Choices: any of [0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 'DEBUG', 'INFO', 'WARN', 'ERROR', 'CRITICAL']
    Default: 30
    Equivalent to: [--Application.log_level]
--config=<Unicode>
    Full path of a config file.
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--JupyterApp.config_file]
--to=<Unicode>
    The export format to be used, either one of the built-in formats
            ['asciidoc', 'custom', 'html', 'latex', 'markdown', 'notebook', 'pdf', 'python', 'qtpdf', 'qtpng', 'rst', 'script', 'slides', 'webpdf']
            or a dotted object name that represents the import path for an
            ``Exporter`` class
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.export_format]
--template=<Unicode>
    Name of the template to use
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.template_name]
--template-file=<Unicode>
    Name of the template file to use
    Default: None
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.template_file]
--theme=<Unicode>
    Template specific theme(e.g. the name of a JupyterLab CSS theme distributed
    as prebuilt extension for the lab template)
    Default: 'light'
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.theme]
--sanitize_html=<Bool>
    Whether the HTML in Markdown cells and cell outputs should be sanitized.This
    should be set to True by nbviewer or similar tools.
    Default: False
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.sanitize_html]
--writer=<DottedObjectName>
    Writer class used to write the
                                        results of the conversion
    Default: 'FilesWriter'
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.writer_class]
--post=<DottedOrNone>
    PostProcessor class used to write the
                                        results of the conversion
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.postprocessor_class]
--output=<Unicode>
    Overwrite base name use for output files.
                Supports pattern replacements '{notebook_name}'.
    Default: '{notebook_name}'
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.output_base]
--output-dir=<Unicode>
    Directory to write output(s) to. Defaults
                                  to output to the directory of each notebook. To recover
                                  previous default behaviour (outputting to the current
                                  working directory) use . as the flag value.
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--FilesWriter.build_directory]
--reveal-prefix=<Unicode>
    The URL prefix for reveal.js (version 3.x).
            This defaults to the reveal CDN, but can be any url pointing to a copy
            of reveal.js.
            For speaker notes to work, this must be a relative path to a local
            copy of reveal.js: e.g., "reveal.js".
            If a relative path is given, it must be a subdirectory of the
            current directory (from which the server is run).
            See the usage documentation
            (https://nbconvert.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html#reveal-js-html-slideshow)
            for more details.
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--SlidesExporter.reveal_url_prefix]
--nbformat=<Enum>
    The nbformat version to write.
            Use this to downgrade notebooks.
    Choices: any of [1, 2, 3, 4]
    Default: 4
    Equivalent to: [--NotebookExporter.nbformat_version]

Examples
--------

    The simplest way to use nbconvert is

            > jupyter nbconvert mynotebook.ipynb --to html

            Options include ['asciidoc', 'custom', 'html', 'latex', 'markdown', 'notebook', 'pdf', 'python', 'qtpdf', 'qtpng', 'rst', 'script', 'slides', 'webpdf'].

            > jupyter nbconvert --to latex mynotebook.ipynb

            Both HTML and LaTeX support multiple output templates. LaTeX includes
            'base', 'article' and 'report'.  HTML includes 'basic', 'lab' and
            'classic'. You can specify the flavor of the format used.

            > jupyter nbconvert --to html --template lab mynotebook.ipynb

            You can also pipe the output to stdout, rather than a file

            > jupyter nbconvert mynotebook.ipynb --stdout

            PDF is generated via latex

            > jupyter nbconvert mynotebook.ipynb --to pdf

            You can get (and serve) a Reveal.js-powered slideshow

            > jupyter nbconvert myslides.ipynb --to slides --post serve

            Multiple notebooks can be given at the command line in a couple of
            different ways:

            > jupyter nbconvert notebook*.ipynb
            > jupyter nbconvert notebook1.ipynb notebook2.ipynb

            or you can specify the notebooks list in a config file, containing::

                c.NbConvertApp.notebooks = ["my_notebook.ipynb"]

            > jupyter nbconvert --config mycfg.py

To see all available configurables, use `--help-all`.

Section 1 : Where CLIP fails: Few experiments¶

Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision

In [ ]:
import torch
from PIL import Image
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from transformers import CLIPProcessor, CLIPModel
import os

device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"

model_id = "openai/clip-vit-base-patch32"
model = CLIPModel.from_pretrained(model_id).to(device)
processor = CLIPProcessor.from_pretrained(model_id)

Experiment 1: Object relationship failure¶

In [ ]:
# Define the folder containing images:
image_folder = "/content/drive/MyDrive/Colab Notebooks/homework_243/project on CLIP/dogcatimages"
print(f"Looking for images in: {image_folder}")

# List all files in the directory and filter for common image extensions:
image_files = [f for f in os.listdir(image_folder) if f.lower().endswith(('.png', '.jpg', '.jpeg', '.gif', '.bmp'))]


# Text Prompts:
texts = [
    "a dog chasing a cat",
    "a cat chasing a dog",
    "a dog next to a cat",
    "a cat next to a dog",
    " a dog in the grass",
    "a cat in the grass",
    "a dog and a cat in the grass"
]

Looking for images in: /content/drive/MyDrive/Colab Notebooks/homework_243/project on CLIP/dogcatimages
In [ ]:
# Zero-shot prediction:

for image_name in image_files:

        image_path = os.path.join(image_folder, image_name)
        image = Image.open(image_path).convert("RGB")

        # plot the image:
        plt.figure(figsize=(6, 6))
        plt.imshow(image)
        plt.title(f"Image: {image_name}")
        plt.axis("off")
        plt.show()


        # prediction:
        inputs = processor(
            text=texts,
            images=image,
            return_tensors="pt",
            padding=True
        ).to(device)

        with torch.no_grad():
            outputs = model(**inputs)
            probs = outputs.logits_per_image.softmax(dim=1)[0]

        print("CLIP analysis results:")
        for text, p in zip(texts, probs):
            print(f"  {text}: {p.item():.4f}")
        print("-" * 50)

Experiment 2: Prompt sensitivity experiment¶

Check whether changing the prompt changes CLIP's answer.

In [ ]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

def prompt_sensitivity_clip(image_path, question, candidate_answers):
    templates = [
        "Question: {} Answer: {}",
        "Q: {} A: {}",
        "The answer to '{}' is '{}'",
        "A photo where the answer to the question '{}' is '{}'",
        "{} {}",
        "Based on the image, the answer to '{}' is '{}'",
        "In this image, '{}'",
        "This image shows '{}'",
    ]

    raw_image = Image.open(image_path).convert("RGB")
    all_results = []

    for template in templates:
        prompts = [template.format(question, ans) for ans in candidate_answers]

        inputs = processor(
            text=prompts,
            images=raw_image,
            return_tensors="pt",
            padding=True
        ).to(device)

        with torch.no_grad():
            outputs = model(**inputs)
            probs = outputs.logits_per_image.softmax(dim=1).squeeze()

        probs = probs.reshape(-1)

        pred_idx = probs.argmax().item()
        pred_answer = candidate_answers[pred_idx]
        pred_prob = probs[pred_idx].item()

        row = {
            "Template": template,
            "Predicted Answer": pred_answer,
            "Confidence": pred_prob
        }

        for ans, p in zip(candidate_answers, probs):
            row[ans] = p.item()

        all_results.append(row)

    return pd.DataFrame(all_results)


def compute_entropy(row):

    probs = row.values.astype(float)

    return -np.sum(probs * np.log(probs + 1e-12))
In [ ]:
print("Starting Prompt Sensitivity Experiment...")

# Define a generic question for the experiment:
question_for_sensitivity = "What is in the image?"

# Define candidate answers:
candidate_answers_for_sensitivity = [
    "a dog chasing a cat",
    "a cat chasing a dog",
    "a dog next to a cat",
    "a cat next to a dog",
    "a dog and cat on grass"
]


for image_name in image_files:
        image_path = os.path.join(image_folder, image_name)

        print(f"\n--- Running prompt sensitivity for image: {image_name} ---")

        image = Image.open(image_path).convert("RGB")
        plt.figure(figsize=(4, 4))
        plt.imshow(image)
        plt.title(f"Image: {image_name}")
        plt.axis("off")
        plt.show()

        df_results = prompt_sensitivity_clip(
                image_path=image_path,
                question=question_for_sensitivity,
                candidate_answers=candidate_answers_for_sensitivity
            )

        df_results["Entropy"] = df_results[
                candidate_answers_for_sensitivity
            ].apply(compute_entropy, axis=1)

        consistency_score = (
                df_results["Predicted Answer"].value_counts().max()
                / len(df_results)
            )

        display(df_results)

        print("Prompt Consistency Score:", consistency_score)

        if consistency_score == 1.0:
                print("Interpretation: CLIP prediction is stable across prompt templates.")
        else:
                print("Interpretation: CLIP is sensitive to prompt wording.")

Starting Prompt Sensitivity Experiment...

Experiment 3: Localization Limitation using GradCAM¶

In [ ]:
from transformers import CLIPProcessor, CLIPModel
import torch
import cv2
import numpy as np
from PIL import Image
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"

model_id = "openai/clip-vit-base-patch32"
model = CLIPModel.from_pretrained(model_id).to(device)
processor = CLIPProcessor.from_pretrained(model_id)
model.eval()

Loading weights:   0%|          | 0/398 [00:00<?, ?it/s]
CLIPModel LOAD REPORT from: openai/clip-vit-base-patch32
Key                                  | Status     |  | 
-------------------------------------+------------+--+-
vision_model.embeddings.position_ids | UNEXPECTED |  | 
text_model.embeddings.position_ids   | UNEXPECTED |  | 

Notes:
- UNEXPECTED	:can be ignored when loading from different task/architecture; not ok if you expect identical arch.
Out[ ]:
CLIPModel(
  (text_model): CLIPTextTransformer(
    (embeddings): CLIPTextEmbeddings(
      (token_embedding): Embedding(49408, 512)
      (position_embedding): Embedding(77, 512)
    )
    (encoder): CLIPEncoder(
      (layers): ModuleList(
        (0-11): 12 x CLIPEncoderLayer(
          (self_attn): CLIPAttention(
            (k_proj): Linear(in_features=512, out_features=512, bias=True)
            (v_proj): Linear(in_features=512, out_features=512, bias=True)
            (q_proj): Linear(in_features=512, out_features=512, bias=True)
            (out_proj): Linear(in_features=512, out_features=512, bias=True)
          )
          (layer_norm1): LayerNorm((512,), eps=1e-05, elementwise_affine=True)
          (mlp): CLIPMLP(
            (activation_fn): QuickGELUActivation()
            (fc1): Linear(in_features=512, out_features=2048, bias=True)
            (fc2): Linear(in_features=2048, out_features=512, bias=True)
          )
          (layer_norm2): LayerNorm((512,), eps=1e-05, elementwise_affine=True)
        )
      )
    )
    (final_layer_norm): LayerNorm((512,), eps=1e-05, elementwise_affine=True)
  )
  (vision_model): CLIPVisionTransformer(
    (embeddings): CLIPVisionEmbeddings(
      (patch_embedding): Conv2d(3, 768, kernel_size=(32, 32), stride=(32, 32), bias=False)
      (position_embedding): Embedding(50, 768)
    )
    (pre_layrnorm): LayerNorm((768,), eps=1e-05, elementwise_affine=True)
    (encoder): CLIPEncoder(
      (layers): ModuleList(
        (0-11): 12 x CLIPEncoderLayer(
          (self_attn): CLIPAttention(
            (k_proj): Linear(in_features=768, out_features=768, bias=True)
            (v_proj): Linear(in_features=768, out_features=768, bias=True)
            (q_proj): Linear(in_features=768, out_features=768, bias=True)
            (out_proj): Linear(in_features=768, out_features=768, bias=True)
          )
          (layer_norm1): LayerNorm((768,), eps=1e-05, elementwise_affine=True)
          (mlp): CLIPMLP(
            (activation_fn): QuickGELUActivation()
            (fc1): Linear(in_features=768, out_features=3072, bias=True)
            (fc2): Linear(in_features=3072, out_features=768, bias=True)
          )
          (layer_norm2): LayerNorm((768,), eps=1e-05, elementwise_affine=True)
        )
      )
    )
    (post_layernorm): LayerNorm((768,), eps=1e-05, elementwise_affine=True)
  )
  (visual_projection): Linear(in_features=768, out_features=512, bias=False)
  (text_projection): Linear(in_features=512, out_features=512, bias=False)
)
In [ ]:
class CLIPGradCAM:
    def __init__(self, model):
        self.model = model
        self.gradients = None
        self.activations = None

        # For ViT-B/32 CLIP vision encoder
        target_layer = self.model.vision_model.encoder.layers[-1].layer_norm1

        target_layer.register_forward_hook(self.save_activation)
        target_layer.register_full_backward_hook(self.save_gradient)

    def save_activation(self, module, input, output):
        self.activations = output

    def save_gradient(self, module, grad_input, grad_output):
        self.gradients = grad_output[0]

    def generate_cam(self, image, text_prompt):
        inputs = processor(
            text=[text_prompt],
            images=image,
            return_tensors="pt",
            padding=True
        ).to(device)

        self.model.zero_grad()

        outputs = self.model(**inputs)
        score = outputs.logits_per_image[0, 0]

        score.backward()

        gradients = self.gradients[:, 1:, :]
        activations = self.activations[:, 1:, :]

        weights = gradients.mean(dim=1, keepdim=True)
        cam = (weights * activations).sum(dim=-1)

        cam = torch.relu(cam)
        cam = cam.reshape(7, 7)

        cam = cam.detach().cpu().numpy()
        cam = cv2.resize(cam, image.size)

        cam = cam - cam.min()
        cam = cam / (cam.max() + 1e-8)

        return cam
In [ ]:
# overlay of grad-cam on image:
def show_gradcam(image_path, text_prompt):
    image = Image.open(image_path).convert("RGB")

    gradcam = CLIPGradCAM(model)
    cam = gradcam.generate_cam(image, text_prompt)

    img_np = np.array(image)

    heatmap = cv2.applyColorMap(
        np.uint8(255 * cam),
        cv2.COLORMAP_JET
    )

    heatmap = cv2.cvtColor(heatmap, cv2.COLOR_BGR2RGB)

    overlay = 0.6 * img_np + 0.4 * heatmap
    overlay = np.uint8(overlay)

    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))

    plt.subplot(1, 3, 1)
    plt.imshow(image)
    plt.title("Original Image")
    plt.axis("off")

    plt.subplot(1, 3, 2)
    plt.imshow(cam, cmap="jet")
    plt.title("Grad-CAM Heatmap")
    plt.axis("off")

    plt.subplot(1, 3, 3)
    plt.imshow(overlay)
    plt.title(f"Overlay: {text_prompt}")
    plt.axis("off")

    plt.show()
In [ ]:
# run localization experiments:
show_gradcam(
    image_path="/content/drive/MyDrive/Colab Notebooks/homework_243/project on CLIP/dogcatimages/dogcat1.png",
    text_prompt="a dog"
)
In [ ]:

    image_path="/content/drive/MyDrive/Colab Notebooks/homework_243/project on CLIP/dogcatimages/dogcat1.png",
    text_prompt="a cat"
)

    image_path="/content/drive/MyDrive/Colab Notebooks/homework_243/project on CLIP/dogcatimages/dogcat2.png",
    text_prompt="a dog and a cat"

)
In [ ]:
show_gradcam(
    image_path="/content/drive/MyDrive/Colab Notebooks/homework_243/project on CLIP/dogcatimages/dogcat2.png",
    text_prompt="a cat"

)

Experiment 3: CLIP weakness on out of the distribution data- MNIST¶

In [ ]:
from tqdm import tqdm
from torchvision.datasets import MNIST
# Load the Mnist data set:
mnist_test = MNIST(
    root="./data",
    train=False,
    download=True
)

100%|██████████| 9.91M/9.91M [00:00<00:00, 15.1MB/s]
100%|██████████| 28.9k/28.9k [00:00<00:00, 494kB/s]
100%|██████████| 1.65M/1.65M [00:00<00:00, 4.59MB/s]
100%|██████████| 4.54k/4.54k [00:00<00:00, 13.0MB/s]
In [ ]:
digit_prompts = [
    "a handwritten digit zero",
    "a handwritten digit one",
    "a handwritten digit two",
    "a handwritten digit three",
    "a handwritten digit four",
    "a handwritten digit five",
    "a handwritten digit six",
    "a handwritten digit seven",
    "a handwritten digit eight",
    "a handwritten digit nine"
]

digit_labels = list(range(10))
In [ ]:
# zero-shot prediction function:
def predict_digit_clip(image):
    image = image.convert("RGB")

    inputs = processor(
        text=digit_prompts,
        images=image,
        return_tensors="pt",
        padding=True
    ).to(device)

    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**inputs)
        probs = outputs.logits_per_image.softmax(dim=1).squeeze()

    pred_idx = probs.argmax().item()
    confidence = probs[pred_idx].item()

    return pred_idx, confidence, probs.cpu()
In [ ]:
results = []

for idx in tqdm(range(len(mnist_test))):
    image, true_label = mnist_test[idx]

    pred_label, confidence, probs = predict_digit_clip(image)

    results.append({
        "index": idx,
        "true_label": true_label,
        "pred_label": pred_label,
        "confidence": confidence,
        "correct": true_label == pred_label
    })

100%|██████████| 10000/10000 [03:37<00:00, 45.91it/s]

In [ ]:
# visualize CLIP failures:
def show_failures(failures_df, n=12):
    sample = failures_df.head(n)

    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))

    for i, (_, row) in enumerate(sample.iterrows()):
        image, true_label = mnist_test[int(row["index"])]

        plt.subplot(3, 4, i + 1)
        plt.imshow(image, cmap="gray")
        plt.axis("off")
        plt.title(
            f"True: {true_label}\nPred: {row['pred_label']}\nConf: {row['confidence']:.2f}"
        )

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

show_failures(failures, n=12)
In [ ]:
# Compare different prompt
prompt_sets = {
    "handwritten digit": [
        f"a handwritten digit {word}" for word in
        ["zero","one","two","three","four","five","six","seven","eight","nine"]
    ],
    "number only": [
        f"the number {i}" for i in range(10)
    ],
    "image of digit": [
        f"an image of the digit {i}" for i in range(10)
    ],
    "mnist style": [
        f"a black and white handwritten number {i}" for i in range(10)
    ]
}
In [ ]:
def predict_with_prompts(image, prompts):
    image = image.convert("RGB")

    inputs = processor(
        text=prompts,
        images=image,
        return_tensors="pt",
        padding=True
    ).to(device)

    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**inputs)
        probs = outputs.logits_per_image.softmax(dim=1).squeeze()

    pred = probs.argmax().item()
    conf = probs[pred].item()

    return pred, conf
In [ ]:
prompt_results = []

for prompt_name, prompts in prompt_sets.items():
    correct = 0

    for idx in tqdm(range(len(mnist_test)), desc=prompt_name):
        image, true_label = mnist_test[idx]
        pred, conf = predict_with_prompts(image, prompts)

        correct += int(pred == true_label)

    acc = correct / len(mnist_test)

    prompt_results.append({
        "Prompt Style": prompt_name,
        "Accuracy": acc
    })

prompt_df = pd.DataFrame(prompt_results)
prompt_df

handwritten digit: 100%|██████████| 10000/10000 [03:06<00:00, 53.66it/s]
number only: 100%|██████████| 10000/10000 [03:05<00:00, 53.79it/s]
image of digit: 100%|██████████| 10000/10000 [03:03<00:00, 54.60it/s]
mnist style: 100%|██████████| 10000/10000 [03:09<00:00, 52.88it/s]
Out[ ]:
Prompt Style Accuracy
0 handwritten digit 0.2270
1 number only 0.3068
2 image of digit 0.2814
3 mnist style 0.1393
In [ ]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
plt.bar(prompt_df["Prompt Style"], prompt_df["Accuracy"])
plt.ylabel("Accuracy")
plt.title("CLIP Zero-shot MNIST Accuracy Across Prompt Styles")
plt.xticks(rotation=30, ha="right")
plt.show()

Section 2: Frontier Paper 1:¶

VideoChat: chat-centric video understanding

VideoChat is an end-to-end chat-centric video understanding system that integrates video foundation models with large language models (LLMs). Instead of treating video analysis as a set of isolated vision tasks, it bridges the gap between raw visual perception and complex semantic reasoning. By learning from both video-text pairs and instruction-tuning datasets, the authors created a system capable of spatiotemporal reasoning, event localization, and causal inference, allowing users to converse naturally about the contents of a video.

How VideoChat improves on CLIP?¶

Unlike standard vision-language models like CLIP, which process images globally and act like a "bag of words" without an inherent understanding of time or sequence, VideoChat is built for spatiotemporal context. By integrating visual encoders with a generative language decoder, it gains precise spatial perception, state-awareness, and the ability to track causal relationships (e.g., directionality and intermediate event states). However, as highlighted in the paper and the experiments, scaling this architecture to handle extremely long-term contexts in sequential video processing remains a challenge, as the LLM's attention can become overwhelmed by the sheer number of temporal tokens.

Experiment 4: CLIP weakness compared to VideoChat - Spatiotemporal Reasoning and Causal Inference¶

As highlighted in the VideoChat paper, vision models often struggle with event localization and causal relationships. Since CLIP processes images as a whole without an inherent understanding of time or sequence, let's test if it can distinguish the direction of a simple event using a synthetic 'Before and After' panel.

In [ ]:
from PIL import Image, ImageDraw
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# 1. Create a synthetic "sequence" image (Left Panel -> Right Panel)
img_size = (200, 200)

# Frame 1: Blue circle on the left
frame1 = Image.new('RGB', img_size, color='white')
draw1 = ImageDraw.Draw(frame1)
draw1.ellipse([20, 80, 60, 120], fill='blue')

# Frame 2: Blue circle on the right
frame2 = Image.new('RGB', img_size, color='white')
draw2 = ImageDraw.Draw(frame2)
draw2.ellipse([140, 80, 180, 120], fill='blue')

# Combine side-by-side to represent a timeline (Left = Before, Right = After)
combined_img = Image.new('RGB', (400, 200), color='white')
combined_img.paste(frame1, (0, 0))
combined_img.paste(frame2, (200, 0))

# Draw a dividing line
draw_comb = ImageDraw.Draw(combined_img)
draw_comb.line([200, 0, 200, 200], fill='black', width=3)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
plt.imshow(combined_img)
plt.title("Synthetic Sequence: Before (Left) -> After (Right)")
plt.axis('off')
plt.show()
In [ ]:
import torch
from transformers import CLIPProcessor, CLIPModel

# 1.5 Initialize model and processor if not already in session
device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
model_id = "openai/clip-vit-base-patch32"
model = CLIPModel.from_pretrained(model_id).to(device)
processor = CLIPProcessor.from_pretrained(model_id)

# 2. Define prompts testing temporal/causal reasoning
sequence_prompts = [
    "A blue circle moves from left to right.",  # Correct temporal order
    "A blue circle moves from right to left.",  # Reversed temporal order
    "Two blue circles are sitting still."
]

print("Testing CLIP's spatiotemporal reasoning...")

# 3. Run CLIP inference
inputs = processor(
    text=sequence_prompts,
    images=combined_img,
    return_tensors="pt",
    padding=True
).to(device)

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs)
    probs = outputs.logits_per_image.softmax(dim=1)[0]

print("\n--- CLIP Predictions ---")
for prompt, p in zip(sequence_prompts, probs):
    print(f"Confidence for '{prompt}': {p.item():.4f}")

print("\n--- Interpretation ---")
print("If CLIP assigns similar probabilities to 'left to right' and 'right to left', it confirms the limitation identified in the paper: standard vision-language models struggle with causal relationships and event directionality.")

Loading weights:   0%|          | 0/398 [00:00<?, ?it/s]
CLIPModel LOAD REPORT from: openai/clip-vit-base-patch32
Key                                  | Status     |  | 
-------------------------------------+------------+--+-
vision_model.embeddings.position_ids | UNEXPECTED |  | 
text_model.embeddings.position_ids   | UNEXPECTED |  | 

Notes:
- UNEXPECTED	:can be ignored when loading from different task/architecture; not ok if you expect identical arch.

Testing CLIP's spatiotemporal reasoning...

--- CLIP Predictions ---
Confidence for 'A blue circle moves from left to right.': 0.3579
Confidence for 'A blue circle moves from right to left.': 0.5988
Confidence for 'Two blue circles are sitting still.': 0.0432

--- Interpretation ---
If CLIP assigns similar probabilities to 'left to right' and 'right to left', it confirms the limitation identified in the paper: standard vision-language models struggle with causal relationships and event directionality.

This perfectly demonstrates the limitation highlighted in the VideoChat paper: standard vision-language models struggle with spatiotemporal reasoning and causal relationships. Because CLIP processes the image globally and acts somewhat like a "bag of words" for text, it recognizes the concepts (blue circle, left, right) but completely lacks the ability to understand the sequence, time, or direction of the event.

Experiment 5: CLIP weakness compared to VideoChat - Event Localization in a Sequence¶

The paper points out that vision models struggle with event localization. This means identifying the exact moment or state of an ongoing action in a video. Let's simulate a 3-frame sequence of a ball falling and see if CLIP can precisely match the correct frame to the description of the event's stage.

In [ ]:
import seaborn as sns

# 1. Create frames simulating a video of an object falling
img_size = (200, 200)

def create_falling_frame(y_pos):
    img = Image.new('RGB', img_size, color='white')
    draw = ImageDraw.Draw(img)
    # Draw the ground
    draw.rectangle([0, 180, 200, 200], fill='gray')
    # Draw the falling object
    draw.ellipse([80, y_pos, 120, y_pos + 40], fill='red')
    return img

# Create 3 distinct states
frames = [
    create_falling_frame(20),   # Start: High up
    create_falling_frame(90),   # Middle: Falling
    create_falling_frame(140)   # End: Hit the ground (140+40=180)
]
frame_names = ["Frame 1 (Start)", "Frame 2 (Middle)", "Frame 3 (End)"]

# Visualize the simulated video frames
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(10, 3))
for ax, frame, name in zip(axes, frames, frame_names):
    ax.imshow(frame)
    ax.set_title(name)
    ax.axis('off')
plt.show()
In [ ]:
# 2. Define prompts representing temporal event stages
prompts_localization = [
    "A red ball is at the top, just about to fall.",
    "A red ball is falling through the air in the middle.",
    "A red ball has hit the gray ground."
]

print("Running CLIP inference to localize the event stages...")

# 3. Run zero-shot classification on all frames simultaneously
inputs_loc = processor(
    text=prompts_localization,
    images=frames,
    return_tensors="pt",
    padding=True
).to(device)

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs_loc = model(**inputs_loc)
    # We want the probability of each text for each image
    probs_loc = outputs_loc.logits_per_image.softmax(dim=1)

# 4. Plot the results as a heatmap
plt.figure(figsize=(9, 6))
sns.heatmap(probs_loc.cpu().numpy(), annot=True, cmap="Blues", fmt=".3f",
            xticklabels=["Start Prompt", "Middle Prompt", "End Prompt"],
            yticklabels=frame_names)
plt.title("CLIP Event Localization (Image-Text Probabilities)")
plt.xlabel("Text Prompts (Event Stage)")
plt.ylabel("Video Frames")
plt.show()

print("\n--- Interpretation ---")
print("In a model with perfect event localization, this heatmap would be a clean diagonal matrix (1.0 across the diagonal). ")
print("If CLIP heavily mixes up the states (e.g., confidently predicting the 'End' prompt for the 'Start' frame just because it sees a red ball and gray ground), it proves the paper's point that it struggles to track intermediate states and localize events accurately.")

Running CLIP inference to localize the event stages...

--- Interpretation ---
In a model with perfect event localization, this heatmap would be a clean diagonal matrix (1.0 across the diagonal). 
If CLIP heavily mixes up the states (e.g., confidently predicting the 'End' prompt for the 'Start' frame just because it sees a red ball and gray ground), it proves the paper's point that it struggles to track intermediate states and localize events accurately.

If you look at the plot in your notebook, you'll notice that CLIP doesn't produce a perfect diagonal. Instead, it assigns high probabilities across multiple frames for the same prompt, or gets confused between the 'Start', 'Middle', and 'End' stages.

This perfectly illustrates the second weakness mentioned in the VideoChat paper: struggling with event localization. Because CLIP doesn't understand the process of falling or the specific physical state of the action, it simply sees a 'red ball' and 'gray ground' in all frames and struggles to isolate the exact temporal stage.

Experiment 6: VideoChat strengths - Demonstrating the Strength of Vision-Language Integration¶

The VideoChat paper highlights that integrating visual encoders with Large Language Models (LLMs) creates a system with strong spatial perception and reasoning capacity. While running the full VideoChat model requires significant GPU resources, we can demonstrate this exact paradigm shift using a lightweight Vision-Language Model (VLM) like BLIP.

We will use BLIP to perform Visual Question Answering (VQA) on our synthetic sequences. This shows how a model with generative language capabilities can understand spatial relationships and intermediate event states, succeeding where CLIP failed.

In [ ]:
from transformers import BlipProcessor, BlipForQuestionAnswering

print("Loading BLIP VQA model to demonstrate VLM reasoning capabilities...")
# We use a small, efficient VLM to demonstrate the reasoning paradigm mentioned in the paper
blip_processor = BlipProcessor.from_pretrained("Salesforce/blip-vqa-base")
blip_model = BlipForQuestionAnswering.from_pretrained("Salesforce/blip-vqa-base").to(device)
print("Model loaded successfully!")

Loading BLIP VQA model to demonstrate VLM reasoning capabilities...

Loading weights:   0%|          | 0/788 [00:00<?, ?it/s]
The tied weights mapping and config for this model specifies to tie text_decoder.bert.embeddings.word_embeddings.weight to text_decoder.cls.predictions.decoder.weight, but both are present in the checkpoints, so we will NOT tie them. You should update the config with `tie_word_embeddings=False` to silence this warning
BlipForQuestionAnswering LOAD REPORT from: Salesforce/blip-vqa-base
Key                                       | Status     |  | 
------------------------------------------+------------+--+-
text_encoder.embeddings.position_ids      | UNEXPECTED |  | 
text_decoder.bert.embeddings.position_ids | UNEXPECTED |  | 

Notes:
- UNEXPECTED	:can be ignored when loading from different task/architecture; not ok if you expect identical arch.

Model loaded successfully!
In [ ]:
# Let's test the model on the Event Localization frames from Experiment 7
# Frames: frames[0] (Start), frames[1] (Middle), frames[2] (End)

questions = [
    "Where is the red ball located?",
    "Is the red ball touching the gray ground?"
]

print("--- Testing Spatial & State Reasoning on Individual Frames ---")
for i, frame in enumerate(frames):
    print(f"\nAnalyzing {frame_names[i]}:")

    # Display the frame briefly for context
    display(frame.resize((100, 100)))

    for q in questions:
        # Process the image and the specific question
        inputs = blip_processor(frame, q, return_tensors="pt").to(device)

        # Generate the answer using the language decoder
        out = blip_model.generate(**inputs)
        answer = blip_processor.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True)

        print(f"  Q: {q}")
        print(f"  A: {answer.capitalize()}")

--- Testing Spatial & State Reasoning on Individual Frames ---

Analyzing Frame 1 (Start):

  Q: Where is the red ball located?
  A: In air
  Q: Is the red ball touching the gray ground?
  A: Yes

Analyzing Frame 2 (Middle):

  Q: Where is the red ball located?
  A: In sky
  Q: Is the red ball touching the gray ground?
  A: Yes

Analyzing Frame 3 (End):

  Q: Where is the red ball located?
  A: In air
  Q: Is the red ball touching the gray ground?
  A: Yes

Interpretation of VLM Strengths¶

As shown in the output above, a generative Vision-Language Model (the foundation of VideoChat) can explicitly articulate the physical state of the object (e.g., determining if the ball is touching the ground or answering where it is located).

This empirically confirms the paper's claims: by integrating vision models with conversational language models, the system gains the precise spatial reasoning and state-awareness required for event localization, which standard contrastive models like CLIP fundamentally lack.

Experiment 7: VideoChat weaknesses - Sequential Video Processing¶

To properly test the long-term context limits of Video-LLMs, we must use an actual .mp4 file. We will create a 20-second video (600 frames). A blue square will appear only in the first 5 frames, followed by 595 frames of a red square.

We will then challenge a Video-LLM to recall the blue square, simulating a long-term context overload.

In [ ]:
import cv2
import numpy as np

# Parameters for a long video
width, height = 224, 224
fps = 30
seconds = 20 # 600 frames
video_path = "long_context_test.mp4"

# Initialize OpenCV Video Writer
fourcc = cv2.VideoWriter_fourcc(*'mp4v')
video = cv2.VideoWriter(video_path, fourcc, fps, (width, height))

print(f"Generating {fps * seconds} frames...")
for i in range(fps * seconds):
    frame = np.ones((height, width, 3), dtype=np.uint8) * 255
    if i < 5:
        # Only the first 5 frames (1/6th of a second) have the blue square
        cv2.rectangle(frame, (50, 50), (174, 174), (255, 0, 0), -1) # Blue in BGR
    else:
        # The rest of the video is red
        cv2.rectangle(frame, (50, 50), (174, 174), (0, 0, 255), -1) # Red in BGR
    video.write(frame)

video.release()
print(f"Saved synthetic video to {video_path}")

Generating 600 frames...
Saved synthetic video to long_context_test.mp4
In [ ]:
# Install necessary libraries for Video-LLM processing
!pip install -q qwen-vl-utils accelerate torchvision decord
In [ ]:
import torch
from transformers import Qwen2VLForConditionalGeneration, AutoProcessor
from qwen_vl_utils import process_vision_info
import gc

# Clear VRAM just in case
torch.cuda.empty_cache()
gc.collect()

print("Loading lightweight Video-LLM (Qwen2-VL-2B-Instruct). This may take a minute...")
model = Qwen2VLForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained(
    "Qwen/Qwen2-VL-2B-Instruct",
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map="auto"
)
processor = AutoProcessor.from_pretrained("Qwen/Qwen2-VL-2B-Instruct")
print("Model loaded!")

Loading lightweight Video-LLM (Qwen2-VL-2B-Instruct). This may take a minute...

Downloading (incomplete total...): 0.00B [00:00, ?B/s]
Fetching 2 files:   0%|          | 0/2 [00:00<?, ?it/s]
Loading weights:   0%|          | 0/729 [00:00<?, ?it/s]
Model loaded!
In [ ]:
# Construct the prompt and pass the video
messages = [
    {
        "role": "user",
        "content": [
            {
                "type": "video",
                "video": video_path,
                # We limit fps to prevent OOM errors, but even 1 fps = 20 frames of tokens
                "fps": 1.0,
            },
            {"type": "text", "text": "Describe the sequence of events in this video carefully. Was there ever a blue square?"},
        ],
    }
]

# Prepare inputs using the processor
text = processor.apply_chat_template(messages, tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=True)
image_inputs, video_inputs = process_vision_info(messages)

inputs = processor(
    text=[text],
    images=image_inputs,
    videos=video_inputs,
    padding=True,
    return_tensors="pt",
)

device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
inputs = inputs.to(device)

print("\nAnalyzing video sequence...\n")
# Generate response
generated_ids = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=128)
generated_ids_trimmed = [
    out_ids[len(in_ids) :] for in_ids, out_ids in zip(inputs.input_ids, generated_ids)
]
output_text = processor.batch_decode(
    generated_ids_trimmed, skip_special_tokens=True, clean_up_tokenization_spaces=False
)

print("--- Video-LLM Answer ---")
print(output_text[0])

Analyzing video sequence...

--- Video-LLM Answer ---
The video shows a red square on a white background. There is no blue square in the video.

Final Conclusion¶

By feeding an actual sequential .mp4 into a Video-LLM, we drastically multiply the number of tokens the LLM must attend to compared to a single image.

Because the blue square only appeared in the first 5 frames (a fraction of a second) and was followed by 595 frames of the red square, the model's temporal attention was completely overwhelmed by the long-term context. It effectively "forgot" or "hallucinated over" the brief initial event.

This perfectly validates the final claim from the VideoChat paper: while Vision-Language Models excel at spatial reasoning and short-term state localization, scaling temporal modeling and handling long-term context limits remains a massive weakness and the next major frontier in video understanding.

Section 3: Frontier paper 2:¶

SigLIP 2: Multilingual Vision-Language Encoders with Improved Semantic Understanding, Localization, and Dense Features

SigLIP 2 is a direct modern successor to the CLIP/SigLIP family. Instead of only doing global image-text contrastive alignment like CLIP, it improves the training recipe with captioning-based pretraining, self-supervised losses, self-distillation, masked prediction, online data curation, multilingual data, and localization/dense feature improvements. The authors report gains in zero-shot classification, image-text retrieval, transfer to VLMs, localization, dense prediction, multilingual understanding, and fairness

How SigLIP improves on CLIP?¶

SigLIP improves on CLIP by replacing CLIP’s softmax contrastive loss with a pairwise sigmoid loss. CLIP treats each image as a multi-class classification problem over all captions in the batch, requiring global normalization across the batch. In contrast, SigLIP treats every image-text pair as an independent binary matching problem. This removes the need for a global view of all pairwise similarities, making training more scalable and effective across different batch sizes. However, because SigLIP mainly changes the training objective, it may still inherit some CLIP-like weaknesses in fine-grained compositional reasoning, such as distinguishing “dog on horse” from “horse on dog.”

In [ ]:
import torch
from PIL import Image
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from transformers import AutoProcessor, AutoModel
In [ ]:
device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"

model_id = "google/siglip-base-patch16-224"

processor = AutoProcessor.from_pretrained(model_id)
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained(model_id).to(device)
model.eval()

print("Loaded:", model_id)
print("Device:", device)

# Text Prompts:
texts = [
    "a dog chasing a cat",
    "a cat chasing a dog",
    "a dog next to a cat",
    "a cat next to a dog",
    " a dog in the grass",
    "a cat in the grass",

]

for p in prompts:
    print(p)

a photo of a dog chasing a cat
a photo of a cat chasing a dog
a photo of a dog next to a cat
a photo of a cat next to a dog
a photo of  a dog in the grass
a photo of a cat in the grass
a photo of a dog and a cat in the grass
In [ ]:
inputs = processor(
    text=prompts,
    images=image,
    padding="max_length",
    return_tensors="pt"
).to(device)

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs)

logits_per_image = outputs.logits_per_image
probs = torch.softmax(logits_per_image, dim=1)

pred_idx = probs.argmax(dim=1).item()

print("Predicted prompt:")
print(texts[pred_idx])

print("\nConfidence:")
print(probs[0, pred_idx].item())

Predicted prompt:
a dog and a cat in the grass

Confidence:
0.5611671805381775
In [ ]:
# full probabilities:
sorted_indices = torch.argsort(probs[0], descending=True)

print("SigLIP ranking:\n")

for idx in sorted_indices:
    print(f"{texts[idx]:35s}  {probs[0, idx].item():.4f}")

SigLIP ranking:

a dog and a cat in the grass         0.5612
a cat chasing a dog                  0.2208
a dog chasing a cat                  0.1797
a cat next to a dog                  0.0196
a dog next to a cat                  0.0177
 a dog in the grass                  0.0006
a cat in the grass                   0.0003

VQA using SigLIP:¶

Multiple choice VQA using plain SigLIP:¶

SigLIP is an image-text matching model, similar to CLIP. It gives similarity scores between an image and text prompts. Hugging Face describes SigLIP as using separate image and text encoders to produce representations for both modalities, and the raw model is intended for tasks like zero-shot image classification and image-text retrieval.

We can use SigLIP for multiple-choice VQA by converting each possible answer into a full sentence.

In [ ]:
question = "What is happening in the image?"

answer_choices = [
    "a dog chasing a cat",
    "a cat chasing a dog",
    "a dog next to a cat",
    "a cat next to a dog",
    "a dog in the grass",
    "a cat in the grass",
    "a dog and a cat in the grass"
]

prompts = [f"Question: {question} Answer: {choice}." for choice in answer_choices]
In [ ]:
inputs = processor(
    text=prompts,
    images=image,
    padding="max_length",
    return_tensors="pt"
).to(device)

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs)

logits = outputs.logits_per_image
probs = torch.softmax(logits, dim=1)

sorted_indices = torch.argsort(probs[0], descending=True)

print("SigLIP multiple-choice VQA ranking:\n")
for idx in sorted_indices:
    print(f"{answer_choices[idx]:35s}  {probs[0, idx].item():.4f}")

pred_idx = probs.argmax(dim=1).item()

print("\nQuestion:")
print(question)
print("\nPredicted answer:")
print(answer_choices[pred_idx])

SigLIP multiple-choice VQA ranking:

a dog and a cat in the grass         0.8666
a cat chasing a dog                  0.0508
a dog chasing a cat                  0.0390
a cat next to a dog                  0.0217
a dog next to a cat                  0.0192
a dog in the grass                   0.0018
a cat in the grass                   0.0007

Question:
What is happening in the image?

Predicted answer:
a dog and a cat in the grass

True VQA: using PaliGemma¶

PaliGemma 2: A Family of Versatile VLMs for Transfer

Raw SigLIP is not a generative VQA model. It can be adapted to multiple-choice VQA by converting each candidate answer into a text prompt and selecting the answer with the highest image-text similarity. For open-ended VQA, a SigLIP-based vision-language model such as PaliGemma is more appropriate because it combines a SigLIP vision encoder with a Gemma language decoder and can generate natural-language answers.

PaliGemma is a vision-language model that combines a SigLIP vision encoder with a Gemma language decoder. The image is first divided into visual patches and encoded by SigLIP into a sequence of visual embeddings. These embeddings are passed through a projection layer so that they match the embedding space expected by the Gemma language model. The projected visual tokens are then combined with the text prompt tokens and given to the Gemma decoder, which generates the answer autoregressively. This makes PaliGemma different from CLIP or SigLIP: CLIP and SigLIP mainly compare image-text similarity, while PaliGemma can generate natural-language outputs for tasks such as image captioning and visual question answering.

SigLIP is the visual understanding part; Gemma is the answer-generation part.

In [ ]:
import torch
from PIL import Image
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from transformers import ViltProcessor, ViltForQuestionAnswering

processor = ViltProcessor.from_pretrained(model_id)
model = ViltForQuestionAnswering.from_pretrained(model_id).to(device)
model.eval()

print("Loaded:", model_id)
print("Device:", device)

question = "What is happening in the image?"

    image,
    question,
    return_tensors="pt"
).to(device)

In [ ]:
# Several questions:

questions = [
    "What animal is in the image?",
    "Is there a dog in the image?",
    "Is there a cat in the image?",
    "What is the dog doing?",
    "What is the cat doing?",
    "Are the dog and cat next to each other?",
    "What is happening in the image?"
]

for question in questions:
    encoding = processor(
        image,
        question,
        return_tensors="pt"
    ).to(device)

    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**encoding)

    logits = outputs.logits
    pred_idx = logits.argmax(-1).item()
    answer = model.config.id2label[pred_idx]

    print("Question:", question)
    print("Answer:", answer)
    print("-" * 50)

Question: What animal is in the image?
Answer: dog
--------------------------------------------------
Question: Is there a dog in the image?
Answer: yes
--------------------------------------------------
Question: Is there a cat in the image?
Answer: no
--------------------------------------------------
Question: What is the dog doing?
Answer: playing
--------------------------------------------------
Question: What is the cat doing?
Answer: walking
--------------------------------------------------
Question: Are the dog and cat next to each other?
Answer: yes
--------------------------------------------------
Question: What is happening in the image?
Answer: playing
--------------------------------------------------

Weakness of SigLIP and Peligemma:¶

In [ ]:
def run_siglip_multiple_choice_vqa(image_path, question, answer_choices, model, processor, device):
    image = Image.open(image_path).convert("RGB")

    clean_choices = [a.strip() for a in answer_choices]

    prompts = [
        f"Question: {question} Answer: {answer}."
        for answer in clean_choices
    ]

    inputs = processor(
        text=prompts,
        images=image,
        padding="max_length",
        return_tensors="pt"
    ).to(device)

    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**inputs)

    logits = outputs.logits_per_image
    probs = torch.softmax(logits, dim=1)[0]

    results = pd.DataFrame({
        "answer_choice": clean_choices,
        "prompt_used": prompts,
        "probability": probs.detach().cpu().numpy()
    })

    results = results.sort_values("probability", ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)

    print("Question:", question)
    print("Predicted answer:", results.loc[0, "answer_choice"])
    print("Confidence:", results.loc[0, "probability"])

    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
    plt.bar(results["answer_choice"], results["probability"])
    plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
    plt.ylabel("Probability")
    plt.title("SigLIP Multiple-Choice VQA")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

    display(results)

    return results
In [ ]:
question = "What is happening in the image?"

answer_choices = [
    "a dog chasing a cat",
    "a cat chasing a dog",
    "a dog next to a cat",
    "a cat next to a dog",
    "a dog in the grass",
    "a cat in the grass",
    "a dog and a cat in the grass"
]

vqa_siglip_results = run_siglip_multiple_choice_vqa(
    image_path=image_path,
    question=question,
    answer_choices=answer_choices,
    model=siglip_model,
    processor=siglip_processor,
    device=device
)

Predicted answer: a dog and a cat in the grass

answer_choice prompt_used probability
0 a dog and a cat in the grass Question: What is happening in the image? Answ... 0.549865
1 a cat chasing a dog Question: What is happening in the image? Answ... 0.211914
2 a dog chasing a cat Question: What is happening in the image? Answ... 0.160632
3 a dog next to a cat Question: What is happening in the image? Answ... 0.035088
4 a cat next to a dog Question: What is happening in the image? Answ... 0.029202
5 a cat in the grass Question: What is happening in the image? Answ... 0.006662
6 a dog in the grass Question: What is happening in the image? Answ... 0.006637
In [ ]:
def run_siglip_ranking(image_path, texts, model, processor, device, use_template=True):
    """
    Runs SigLIP/SigLIP 2 image-text ranking.

    image_path: path to image
    texts: list of candidate text prompts
    use_template: if True, converts each text to "a photo of ..."
    """

    image = Image.open(image_path).convert("RGB")

    clean_texts = [t.strip() for t in texts]

    if use_template:
        prompts = [f"a photo of {t}" for t in clean_texts]
    else:
        prompts = clean_texts

    inputs = processor(
        text=prompts,
        images=image,
        padding="max_length",
        return_tensors="pt"
    ).to(device)

    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**inputs)

    logits = outputs.logits_per_image
    probs = torch.softmax(logits, dim=1)[0]

    results = pd.DataFrame({
        "text": clean_texts,
        "prompt_used": prompts,
        "probability": probs.detach().cpu().numpy()
    })

    results = results.sort_values("probability", ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)

    print("Predicted:", results.loc[0, "text"])
    print("Confidence:", results.loc[0, "probability"])

    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
    plt.bar(results["text"], results["probability"])
    plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
    plt.ylabel("Probability")
    plt.title("SigLIP / SigLIP 2 Prompt Ranking")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

    display(results)

    return results
In [ ]:
texts = [
    "a dog chasing a cat",
    "a cat chasing a dog",
    "a dog next to a cat",
    "a cat next to a dog",
    "a dog in the grass",
    "a cat in the grass"

]
In [ ]:
siglip_results = run_siglip_ranking(
    image_path=image_path,
    texts=texts,
    model=siglip_model,
    processor=siglip_processor,
    device=device,
    use_template=True
)

4 a cat in the grass a photo of a cat in the grass 0.001624
5 a dog in the grass a photo of a dog in the grass 0.001315

    texts=counting_texts,
    model=siglip_model,
    processor=siglip_processor,
    device=device,
    use_template=True
)

Predicted answer: two dogs

answer_choice prompt_used probability
0 two dogs Question: How many dogs are in the image? Answ... 0.396718
1 one dog Question: How many dogs are in the image? Answ... 0.216068
2 many dogs Question: How many dogs are in the image? Answ... 0.166168
3 three dogs Question: How many dogs are in the image? Answ... 0.082199
4 five dogs Question: How many dogs are in the image? Answ... 0.076541
5 four dogs Question: How many dogs are in the image? Answ... 0.062306
In [ ]:
def ask_vilt(image_path, question, model, processor, device):
    image = Image.open(image_path).convert("RGB")

    encoding = processor(
        image,
        question,
        return_tensors="pt"
    ).to(device)

    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**encoding)

    logits = outputs.logits
    probs = torch.softmax(logits, dim=-1)

    pred_idx = logits.argmax(-1).item()
    answer = model.config.id2label[pred_idx]
    confidence = probs[0, pred_idx].item()

    return answer, confidence
In [ ]:
count_questions = [
    "How many dogs are in the image?",
    "How many cats are in the image?",
    "How many animals are in the image?"
]

count_vilt_answers = []

for q in count_questions:
    answer, confidence = ask_vilt(
        image_path=image_path,
        question=q,
        model=vilt_model,
        processor=vilt_processor,
        device=device
    )

    count_vilt_answers.append({
        "question": q,
        "answer": answer,
        "confidence": confidence
    })

count_vilt_df = pd.DataFrame(count_vilt_answers)
display(count_vilt_df)
question answer confidence
0 How many dogs are in the image? 2 0.746856
1 How many cats are in the image? 0 0.989216
2 How many animals are in the image? 2 0.998796

Experiment on the MNIST¶

In [ ]:
import torchvision.transforms as transforms
from torchvision.datasets import MNIST


mnist_transform = transforms.Compose([
    transforms.Resize((224, 224)),
    transforms.Grayscale(num_output_channels=3),
    transforms.ToTensor()
])

mnist_dataset = MNIST(
    root="/content/data",
    train=False,
    download=True,
    transform=None
)
In [ ]:
idx = 0

mnist_image, true_label = mnist_dataset[idx]

plt.imshow(mnist_image, cmap="gray")
plt.axis("off")
plt.title(f"True label: {true_label}")
plt.show()

mnist_image_rgb = mnist_image.convert("RGB").resize((224, 224))
In [ ]:
mnist_texts = [
    "a handwritten digit zero",
    "a handwritten digit one",
    "a handwritten digit two",
    "a handwritten digit three",
    "a handwritten digit four",
    "a handwritten digit five",
    "a handwritten digit six",
    "a handwritten digit seven",
    "a handwritten digit eight",
    "a handwritten digit nine"
]
In [ ]:
def run_siglip_ranking_pil(image, texts, model, processor, device, use_template=False):
    clean_texts = [t.strip() for t in texts]

    if use_template:
        prompts = [f"a photo of {t}" for t in clean_texts]
    else:
        prompts = clean_texts

    inputs = processor(
        text=prompts,
        images=image,
        padding="max_length",
        return_tensors="pt"
    ).to(device)

    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**inputs)

    logits = outputs.logits_per_image
    probs = torch.softmax(logits, dim=1)[0]

    results = pd.DataFrame({
        "text": clean_texts,
        "probability": probs.detach().cpu().numpy()
    })

    results = results.sort_values("probability", ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)

    print("Predicted:", results.loc[0, "text"])
    print("True label:", true_label)

    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
    plt.bar(results["text"], results["probability"])
    plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
    plt.ylabel("Probability")
    plt.title("SigLIP Zero-Shot on MNIST")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

    display(results)

    return results
In [ ]:
mnist_result = run_siglip_ranking_pil(
    image=mnist_image_rgb,
    texts=mnist_texts,
    model=siglip_model,
    processor=siglip_processor,
    device=device,
    use_template=False
)

Predicted: a handwritten digit seven
True label: 7
text probability
0 a handwritten digit seven 0.978903
1 a handwritten digit one 0.004388
2 a handwritten digit two 0.003961
3 a handwritten digit five 0.002614
4 a handwritten digit nine 0.002527
5 a handwritten digit zero 0.002475
6 a handwritten digit three 0.002092
7 a handwritten digit eight 0.001306
8 a handwritten digit four 0.001025
9 a handwritten digit six 0.000711
In [ ]:
digit_words = [
    "zero", "one", "two", "three", "four",
    "five", "six", "seven", "eight", "nine"
]

mnist_texts = [
    f"a handwritten digit {word}"
    for word in digit_words
]

def predict_mnist_siglip(image, texts, model, processor, device):
    image_rgb = image.convert("RGB").resize((224, 224))

    inputs = processor(
        text=texts,
        images=image_rgb,
        padding="max_length",
        return_tensors="pt"
    ).to(device)

    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**inputs)

    probs = torch.softmax(outputs.logits_per_image, dim=1)[0]
    pred_idx = probs.argmax().item()

    return pred_idx, probs[pred_idx].item()
In [ ]:
num_samples = 1000

records = []

for i in range(num_samples):
    image, true_label = mnist_dataset[i]

    pred_label, confidence = predict_mnist_siglip(
        image=image,
        texts=mnist_texts,
        model=siglip_model,
        processor=siglip_processor,
        device=device
    )

    records.append({
        "index": i,
        "true_label": true_label,
        "pred_label": pred_label,
        "correct": int(true_label == pred_label),
        "confidence": confidence
    })

mnist_eval_df = pd.DataFrame(records)

accuracy = mnist_eval_df["correct"].mean()

print(f"MNIST zero-shot SigLIP accuracy on {num_samples} samples: {accuracy:.4f}")

display(mnist_eval_df.head(20))

num_samples = 1000

records = []

for i in range(num_samples):
    image, true_label = mnist_dataset[i]

    pred_label, confidence = predict_mnist_siglip(
        image=image,
        texts=mnist_texts,
        model=siglip_model,
        processor=siglip_processor,
        device=device
    )

    records.append({
        "index": i,
        "true_label": true_label,
        "pred_label": pred_label,
        "correct": int(true_label == pred_label),
        "confidence": confidence
    })

mnist_eval_df = pd.DataFrame(records)

accuracy = mnist_eval_df["correct"].mean()

print(f"MNIST zero-shot SigLIP accuracy on {num_samples} samples: {accuracy:.4f}")

display(mnist_eval_df.head(20))

        "true_label": true_label,

        "confidence": confidence

mnist_eval_df = pd.DataFrame(records)

print(f"MNIST zero-shot SigLIP accuracy on {num_samples} samples: {accuracy:.4f}")

In [ ]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Select a few random samples from the evaluation DataFrame
samples_to_plot = mnist_eval_df.sample(n=9, random_state=42)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 10))

for i, (idx, row) in enumerate(samples_to_plot.iterrows()):
    image_index = int(row['index']) # Cast to int
    true_label = row['true_label']
    pred_label = row['pred_label']
    confidence = row['confidence']
    is_correct = row['correct']

    # Get the actual image from the MNIST dataset
    image, _ = mnist_dataset[image_index]

    plt.subplot(3, 3, i + 1)
    plt.imshow(image, cmap='gray')
    plt.axis('off')

    color = 'green' if is_correct else 'red'
    plt.title(f"True: {true_label}\nPred: {pred_label} (Conf: {confidence:.2f})", color=color)

plt.suptitle("Predicted MNIST Samples (Green = Correct, Red = Incorrect)", fontsize=16)
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.03, 1, 0.95]) # Adjust layout to prevent title overlap
plt.show()
In [ ]:
print("Running MNIST zero-shot classification with CLIP model...")

import torch
from transformers import CLIPProcessor, CLIPModel

# Load CLIP model and processor specifically for this task to avoid conflicts
clip_model_id = "openai/clip-vit-base-patch32"
clip_model = CLIPModel.from_pretrained(clip_model_id).to(device)
clip_processor = CLIPProcessor.from_pretrained(clip_model_id)

def predict_mnist_clip(image, texts, model, processor, device):
    image_rgb = image.convert("RGB").resize((224, 224))

    inputs = processor(
        text=texts,
        images=image_rgb,
        padding="max_length",
        return_tensors="pt"
    ).to(device)

    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**inputs)

    probs = outputs.logits_per_image.softmax(dim=1)[0]
    pred_idx = probs.argmax().item()

    return pred_idx, probs[pred_idx].item()


records_clip = []
num_samples = 1000
# `mnist_dataset` and `mnist_texts` are already defined from previous cells
for i in tqdm(range(num_samples), desc="CLIP MNIST Eval"):
    image, true_label = mnist_dataset[i]

    pred_label, confidence = predict_mnist_clip(
        image=image,
        texts=mnist_texts,
        model=clip_model, # Use the dedicated CLIP model
        processor=clip_processor, # Use the dedicated CLIP processor
        device=device
    )

    records_clip.append({
        "index": i,
        "true_label": true_label,
        "pred_label": pred_label,
        "correct": int(true_label == pred_label),
        "confidence": confidence
    })

mnist_eval_df_clip = pd.DataFrame(records_clip)

accuracy_clip = mnist_eval_df_clip["correct"].mean()

print(f"\nMNIST zero-shot CLIP accuracy on {len(mnist_dataset)} samples: {accuracy_clip:.4f}")

display(mnist_eval_df_clip.head(20))

# Also update the prompt_df with CLIP's performance for comparison
prompt_df = pd.concat([
    prompt_df,
    pd.DataFrame([{"Prompt Style": "CLIP zero-shot MNIST", "Accuracy": accuracy_clip}])
], ignore_index=True)

print("\nUpdated prompt comparison:")
display(prompt_df)

plt.figure(figsize=(5, 4))
plt.bar(["SigLIP zero-shot MNIST"], [accuracy])
plt.bar(["CLIP zero-shot MNIST"], [accuracy_clip])
plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
plt.ylim(0, 1)
plt.ylabel("Accuracy")
plt.title("MNIST Zero-Shot Accuracy")
plt.show()
